subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

files.fileName: Sofia Santecchia Econ Unit 2 IA Final.pdf

strengths: Provides three clearly drawn, fully labelled diagrams (negative externality, tax shift, PED-adjusted outcome) following IB conventions
Each diagram is accompanied by a step-by-step, accurate explanation linking curve movements to article facts (tax rate changes, inelastic demand, welfare loss reduction)
weaknesses: Figures 2â4 are split across separate images, which may confuse the reader; consolidating them would improve cohesion
Diagram captions occasionally omit explicit labels for welfare-loss areas (e.g. ABC) and specific tax-rate annotations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets all top-band requirements: relevant, accurate, correctly labelled diagrams and full, accurate explanations. Minor organisational and labelling suggestions do not detract from overall excellence, warranting the full 3 marks.

strengths: Consistent and precise use of economic terminology throughout (e.g. negative consumption externality, allocative inefficiency, PED, asymmetric information)
Terminology is applied correctly in context, enhancing clarity and depth of analysis
weaknesses: Classification of junk food as an inferior good lacks empirical justification
Inconsistent formatting (e.g. LaTeX for MPB > MSB) could be standardized for mathematical clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Terminology is used appropriately and consistently, satisfying the highest descriptor. Minor criticisms relate to justification and formatting, but do not undermine the correct and pervasive use of economic vocabulary, justifying the full 2 marks.

strengths: Thorough application of economic theory frames every section, from externality identification to tax internalisation and information failure
Analysis is effective and well-developed: explores welfare implications, elasticity-driven limitations, equity issues, and health system benefits with logical, evidence-based chains of reasoning
weaknesses: Claims about PED could be bolstered by specific empirical estimates or sources
Welfare-loss area (ABC) could be defined more explicitly in the narrative to deepen analytical clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary demonstrates the highest level of application and analysis: theory is thoroughly applied and analysis is well developed. Suggestions for empirical support and deeper boundaries do not diminish the strong performance, meriting the full 3 marks.

strengths: Key concept âInterventionâ is clearly and appropriately identified on the cover page and integrated throughout
Full, clear connection between the concept and the articleâs content (tax and labelling policies addressing externalities and information failure)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student explicitly states and applies the key concept at the highest level, with a full and clear explanation of its link to the article. No substantive areas for improvement, justifying the full 3 marks.

strengths: Provides effective, balanced judgments on policy effectiveness, equity impacts, and complementary measures
Each judgment is supported by explicit, logical economic reasoning (PED analysis, distributional consequences, long-run health benefits)
weaknesses: Long-term health and economic wellbeing claims would benefit from specific data or external study citations
Numerical elasticity estimates could strengthen the evaluative reasoning
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation is balanced and well-supported, aligning with top-band descriptors. Suggestions relate to empirical backing rather than the quality of judgments or reasoning, thus warranting the full 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Analysis moves beyond diagram description to explore welfare implications, elasticity-driven shortcomings, distributional issues for low-income households and long-run health system benefits. The chain of reasoning is logical and supported by evidence from the article, demonstrating effective and well-developed analysis rather than superficial treatment.

The student provides three clearly-drawn diagrams (negative consumption externality, partial internalisation with 10 % tax, and PED-adjusted outcome). Axes, curves (MSB, MPB, MSC, S, S1, S2), equilibrium points, welfare-loss areas and price/quantity indicators are all correctly labelled and correspond with standard IB conventions. No technical inaccuracies or omissions are evident.

The student clearly links the concept of intervention to the Colombian junk-food law, explaining how the tax and front-of-pack labels correct the externality and information failure. The explanation is integrated with article details (timing, percentage rates, industry exemptions), giving a full and clear connection.

Economic theory underpins every paragraph: identification of the externality, internalisation via indirect tax, PED limits, information failure, income effects and equity concerns. Theory is not merely mentioned; it frames the whole argument and is applied to multiple dimensions of the same policy, meeting the standard of being "thoroughly applied throughout."

Each judgement is backed by economic reasoning: PED analysis for effectiveness, inferior good argument for income effects, long-run public health cost savings for societal benefit. Reasoning is explicit and logically supports the evaluative statements, matching the top-band descriptor.

The key concept âInterventionâ is explicitly stated in the cover page and repeatedly referenced in the commentary when describing the tax and labelling policies. Its choice is appropriate to a government health tax and has not been used in another commentary, satisfying the highest descriptor.

For each figure the student gives a step-by-step narrative that links movements of curves to the articleâs facts (10 % rising to 20 % tax, PED effects, welfare loss removal). Explanations explicitly reference how the new equilibrium reduces over-consumption and why the outcome changes when demand is inelastic, demonstrating a full and accurate grasp rather than a cursory mention.

Throughout the commentary the student consistently uses precise microeconomic vocabularyânegative consumption externality, allocative inefficiency, ceteris paribus, PED, asymmetric information, inferior/normal goods, VAT, welfare lossâcorrectly and in context. No noticeable misuse is present, satisfying the descriptor for appropriate use "throughout".

Judgements are provided on effectiveness (elasticity limitations), equity (impact on low-income households) and possible policy complements (reducing VAT on healthy food). The commentary weighs benefits against drawbacks, reflecting balanced evaluation rather than one-sided assertions.

annotations.comment: The term âasymmetric informationâ is used precisely in context, reinforcing the analysis of how labeling complements the tax intervention.
Link claims about long-term health system savings and economic wellbeing to specific article data or external studies to bolster evaluative reasoning.
Figure 2 appears split into two separate images (Figures 3 and 4); consolidating them into one cohesive diagram would reduce confusion.
The argument on inelastic demand would benefit from citing a specific PED estimate or empirical source to support the claim of low responsiveness.
The student clearly defines the key concept âInterventionâ and situates it appropriately in the context of market failure, demonstrating a robust understanding of the criterion.
Figure 1 is drawn with correct axes and curves (MSB, MPB, MSC) and labels follow IB conventions accurately, supporting clear visual analysis.
The caption for Figure 1 could specify which curves correspond to MPB and MSC, improving reader clarity on the diagramâs components.
The narrative mentions deadweight loss area ABC but does not define its boundaries or explain its significance fully; elaborating on how ABC arises would strengthen the analysis.
The explanation links the 10%â20% rising tax clearly to supply shifts and price changes, effectively applying theory to article facts.
The supplyâshift diagram is accurately drawn with distinct S, S1, S2 curves reflecting the imposition of the tax, enhancing visual impact.
Discussing numerical elasticity estimates (e.g.\, a PED coefficient) would strengthen the evaluation of priceâresponse rather than relying solely on qualitative inferences.
The inelasticâdemand diagram in Figure 3 effectively illustrates limited quantity reduction, reinforcing the discussion of PED constraints.
The initial paragraph uses precise terms like ânegative consumption externalitiesâ and âallocative inefficiency,â showcasing strong use of economic vocabulary.
Discussing potential impacts on producer surplus when the supply curve shifts would deepen the welfare analysis beyond consumer effects.
Classifying junk food as an inferior good is asserted without empirical justification; the student should reference data or clarify the conditions under which this holds.
Suggest reviewing the concept: goods do not âtransitionâ from inferior to normal based on price changes; instead, demand shifts should be linked to income effects explicitly.
The equity evaluation of regressive impacts on lowâincome households is balanced and demonstrates effective judgment of distributional consequences.
Longârun claims regarding life expectancy and local business demand are unsubstantiated; linking these to empirical studies or article evidence would strengthen the evaluation.
While the inequality MPB > MSB is correct, consistent use of LaTeX formatting (e.g., $$MPB > MSB$$) would enhance mathematical clarity.
Integration of the articleâs mention of health labels with the inelasticâdemand diagram shows strong application of theory to address information failure.
Caption for Figure 1 does not name the welfareâloss area explicitly; labeling ABC directly on the diagram would improve immediacy of reference.
Augment Figure 2 by annotating specific taxârate points (10%, 20%) on the supply curves to visually link rate changes to curve shifts.
Including a combined diagram showing both tax and label effects on demand would enhance the application by depicting the cumulative market shifts.
